meta.title: Pressure Drop in Pipes (Darcy-Weisbach Guide)
meta.description: Learn how to calculate pressure loss in fluid systems using the Darcy-Weisbach equation. Understand the roles of friction factor and Reynolds number.

hero.intro:
Fluid transport requires energy to overcome internal friction. Pressure drop calculation is the cornerstone of pump sizing and piping system design.

Pumps are sized on flow and head — head includes friction losses in pipe, fittings, and valves. Darcy–Weisbach relates pressure drop to friction factor, geometry, and velocity head. Reynolds number selects laminar vs turbulent correlations.

The Fluid Flow engine in AluCalc evaluates the same ΔP = f·(L/D)·ρV²/2 relationship for line sizing.

stepByStep: Calculate the Reynolds number to determine flow type (laminar vs turbulent).
Find the friction factor (f) using the Moody chart or equations.
Account for local losses (valves, elbows) if applicable.
Compute Reynolds number Re = ρVD/μ to select friction factor.
Sum minor losses from fittings using equivalent length or K factors.
whyThisMatters: Inadequate pump sizing leads to system failure or insufficient flow.
Excessive pressure drop results in high energy consumption and piping wear.
commonMistakes: Ignoring the effect of pipe roughness on the friction factor.
Assuming laminar flow when it is actually turbulent.
Failing to convert units correctly (e.g., bar to Pascal).
engineeringTip: In turbulent flow, doubling the velocity increases the pressure drop by a factor of four.

calculatorCta.label: Use the Pipe Flow Tool to calculate losses in complex piping networks

faq.question: What is the Reynolds number?
faq.answer: It is a dimensionless quantity that helps predict flow patterns in fluid flow situations.

faq.question: What is the velocity head?
faq.answer: ρV²/2 — kinetic energy per unit volume; doubling velocity quadruples dynamic pressure.

faq.question: How does pipe roughness affect f?
faq.answer: Higher relative roughness ε/D increases friction factor, especially in turbulent regime.

faq.question: Why size pipe diameter early?
faq.answer: Diameter sets velocity and ΔP — undersized pipe wastes pump energy; oversized adds cost.

learningObjectives: Apply Darcy–Weisbach to relate friction factor, geometry, and velocity head
Estimate Reynolds number to select laminar vs turbulent friction correlations
Size pumps using pressure drop and required flow rate
keyTakeaways: ΔP scales with f·(L/D)·ρV²/2 — velocity dominates in small pipes
Larger diameter cuts velocity quadratically for the same flow
Always verify NPSH after calculating line losses
